stories.title: SoHo Leads U.S. in Trophy Retail Transactions
stories.excerpt: SoHo has emerged as the nation’s most active trophy retail market, according to Adirondack Capital Partners’ (ACP) latest U.S. High Street Retail Capital Markets Report. Drawing on transaction activity tracked during the firm’s most recent reporting period, ACP found that SoHo accounted for 43% of all trophy retail transactions nationwide, underscoring a broader shift toward quality and selectivity as buyers navigate an increasingly disciplined investment environment. “The data points to a clear theme: capital remains available, but it’s increasingly concentrated in the country’s most desirable retail corridors,” said Michael Hunter Coghill, founder and managing partner of Adirondack Capital Partners. “Buyers are underwriting more carefully, but they continue to demonstrate tremendous conviction when it comes to premier assets occupied by enduring brands.” He continued, “Trophy retail has proven itself to be one of the most resilient and defensible sectors in commercial real estate. The market has entered a new phase defined by conviction rather than opportunism.” The post SoHo Leads U.S. in Trophy Retail Transactions appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: SoHo accounts for 43% of U.S. trophy retail transactions in latest period.
Capital increasingly concentrated in premier corridors; buyers underwriting more carefully.
Trophy retail proves resilient; market defined by conviction, not opportunism.
stories.summary: SoHo has captured 43% of all trophy retail transactions nationwide, signaling a flight to quality in an increasingly disciplined investment environment. Capital remains available but is concentrating in the country's most desirable retail corridors, where buyers demonstrate strong conviction in enduring brands and defensible assets. The market has shifted from opportunistic buying to disciplined underwriting focused on premier locations and long-term fundamentals.
stories.impact: Limited direct relevance to multifamily development. However, trophy retail concentration in top-tier markets (e.g., SoHo/NYC) signals continued investor confidence in urban cores, which can support mixed-use development and higher rents in those densifying neighborhoods—but this is a secondary effect and applies mainly to retail-heavy mixed-use projects, not standalone apartments.

stories.title: C&C Development Opens Affordable Housing in Irvine
stories.excerpt: C&C Development, in partnership with Riverside Charitable Corporation, announced the opening of Cartwright Family Apartments, a 60-unit affordable housing community on 1.6 acres at 17861 Cartwright Rd. in the Irvine Business Complex. The community is for households earning between 30% and 80% of the Orange County area median income (AMI), with a portion of units set aside for veterans. Cartwright also includes a leasing preference for current Irvine residents or persons employed in the city. At its opening, Cartwright is 100% occupied. For C&C Development, Cartwright is the firm’s second affordable community in Irvine. This latest development further supports Irvine policies that have made the city a model for diversity, inclusion, and housing access. “This new community provides for a wide range of opportunities for families,” said Todd Cottle, principal, C&C Development. “We know how expensive housing can be, and by providing affordability, these individuals and families will benefit from Irvine amenities, including an outstanding school district. We hope Cartwright is a launch pad for their future.” The post C&C Development Opens Affordable Housing in Irvine appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: C&C Development opened 60-unit affordable community in Irvine Business Complex, 100% occupied.
Units serve 30–80% AMI households; includes veteran set-asides and local preference.
Second affordable project by C&C in Irvine; strong Irvine policies support development pipeline.
stories.summary: C&C Development opened Cartwright Family Apartments, a 60-unit affordable property in Irvine's Business Complex serving households at 30–80% AMI, with veteran units and local employment/residency preference. The property achieved 100% occupancy at opening. This is C&C's second affordable development in Irvine, reflecting the city's robust affordable housing policies and demand.
stories.impact: Irvine's proactive affordable housing policies and strong occupancy signal robust demand and developer confidence in the Orange County market. For multifamily developers, Irvine's combination of local tax incentives, workforce housing policies, and employment draw (Business Complex proximity) supports higher-density rental development; instant stabilization at Cartwright indicates supply shortage relative to demand in that submarket.

stories.title: Sky Equity Tops Out on Upper East Side Condos
stories.excerpt: Sky Equity Group, the New York City-based real estate development firm founded by Simon Dushinsky, has topped out construction on 260 E. 72nd St. on Manhattan’s Upper East Side. The 60-unit condominium project is Sky Equity’s first condo development in Manhattan. Sales are scheduled to launch this fall and will be handled by Douglas Elluman Development Marketing. “This is an important moment for Sky Equity Group as we continue to expand our presence in Manhattan’s luxury condominium market,” said Simon Dushinsky, founder and CEO of Sky Equity Group. “The development reflects our commitment to delivering exceptional homes that stand the test of time and are defined by thoughtful architecture, quality craftsmanship, and enduring value. We look forward to launching sales this fall and continuing to grow our footprint in some of the country’s most dynamic residential markets.” Following a restructuring and recapitalization completed in 2024, Sky Equity Group assumed the role of lead developer for 260 E. 72nd. The site was previously overseen by Chetrit Group. The post Sky Equity Tops Out on Upper East Side Condos appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Sky Equity Group topped out 60-unit condo tower at 260 E. 72nd St, Upper East Side.
First Manhattan condo project for Sky Equity post-2024 recapitalization.
Sales launch fall 2024; project previously under Chetrit Group before restructuring.
stories.summary: Sky Equity Group topped out a 60-unit luxury condo development at 260 E. 72nd Street on the Upper East Side—the firm's first Manhattan condo project. Following a 2024 recapitalization and restructuring, Sky Equity assumed development from Chetrit Group. Sales are scheduled to launch in fall 2024, handled by Douglas Elluman Development Marketing.
stories.impact: Modest relevance to multifamily; this is a condo conversion/for-sale project. However, successful high-rise residential delivery on the Upper East Side (a constrained supply market) signals underlying demand strength. The recapitalization and project continuity after ownership change suggest capital availability for Manhattan residential—useful context for mixed-use developers considering NYC multifamily components, though rental apartment demand signals would be more directly applicable.

stories.title: Sea Breeze Properties Opens Rental Project in San Marcos’ North City District
stories.excerpt: Sea Breeze Properties, joined by civic and business leaders, residents, and community members, recently celebrated the grand opening of 222 North City, a new residential project at the center of San Marcos’ evolving North City district. Designed by Safdie Rabines Architects, the 12-story community combines contemporary architecture, hospitality-inspired living, public art, and community-driven design. “We wanted to create something that felt culturally alive, not just another residential project,”said Darren Levitt, principal of Sea Breeze Properties. “The vision for North City has always been about creating connections through design, walkability, art, food, and shared experiences. 222 reflects that philosophy in a meaningful way.” Residences range from studios to three-bedroom homes spanning 349 to 1,828 square feet. For those seeking a more elevated experience, 128 Collection residences on the higher floors offer premium finishes and additional benefits. 222 North City incorporates more than an acre of open space, with a resort-style pool, two spas, two clubhouses, outdoor pavilions andkitchens, and sun decks. The post Sea Breeze Properties Opens Rental Project in San Marcos’ North City District appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Sea Breeze Properties opened 222 North City, 12-story rental in San Marcos' North City district.
Studios to 3-bed units (349–1,828 SF); 128 premium Collection units; 1+ acre amenities.
Contemporary architecture; walkability, art, and hospitality-driven placemaking focus.
stories.summary: Sea Breeze Properties opened 222 North City, a 12-story multifamily rental in San Marcos' emerging North City district. The community features studios through 3-bedroom units, a 128-unit premium Collection tier with elevated finishes, and more than an acre of resort-style amenities including pools, spas, and shared dining spaces. Design emphasizes walkability, art, and community-driven placemaking.
stories.impact: San Marcos' North City district is establishing itself as a lifestyle-oriented residential growth corridor. The project's strong placemaking focus and premium tier positioning signal developer confidence in the submarket's ability to support higher-rent, amenity-rich product. Successful opening in San Diego County indicates continued inflow of residents and willingness to pay for experience-driven housing; relevant for premium rental development pipeline in Southern California infill submarkets.

stories.title: Apollo, Affinius Capital, RXR Invest $785M in Debt and Equity to Build 175 Third Street
stories.excerpt: One of Brooklyn’s biggest developments just secured a hefty sum of construction financing. Charney Companies, Tavros and Incoco Capital have secured $785 million in debt and equity financing to build 175 Third Street, a 1.1 million-square-foot mixed-use residential complex in Brooklyn that will be the fifth building in the Gowanus Wharf campus, Commercial Observer can first report. Apollo and Affinius Capital have provided $600 million in debt while development partner RXR will provide $185 million in equity. A JLL Capital Markets team of Christopher Peck, Peter Rotchford and Nicco Lupo arranged the transaction. Ben Gray, a partner at Apollo, noted in a statement that the new mixed-use, multifamily development brings housing units to a Gowanus neighborhood that is both growing quickly and displays “strong fundamentals” in the residential space. “The New York City housing market remains undersupplied, and we are pleased to provide a scaled financing solution to support a sponsorship team with deep local market expertise as they execute their broader vision for Gowanus Wharf,” Gray said. To say Charney Companies and Tavros deep local market expertise undersells the group. The two development and property management firms have been constructing assets and units across the Gowanus Wharf since 2019 and are responsible for 585 Union Street, 251 Douglass Street, and the two-building Nevins Landing complex, which together will add more than 1,100 units to the neighborhood. 175 Third Street is the fifth building developed by Charney Companies and Tavros over the last seven years, a development timeline that presages the passing of the Gowanus rezoning from the New York City Council by two years. This landmark plan pushed by the de Blasio administration allowed for an 82-block area around the Gowanus Canal to be upzoned for more than 8,500 new residential units. “When we bought the Union and Douglass [sites], the rezoning hadn’t been completed yet, so we essentially came into
bullets: Charney Companies/Tavros/Incoco secured $785M (600M debt, 185M equity) for 175 Third St, Brooklyn.
Apollo and Affinius provided debt; RXR contributed equity; 1.1M SF mixed-use residential complex.
Fifth building in Gowanus Wharf campus; strong residential fundamentals cited by lender.
stories.summary: Charney Companies, Tavros, and Incoco Capital secured $785 million in construction financing for 175 Third Street, a 1.1 million-square-foot mixed-use residential project in Brooklyn's Gowanus neighborhood. Apollo and Affinius Capital provided $600 million in debt financing; development partner RXR contributed $185 million in equity. The project is the fifth building in the Gowanus Wharf campus, addressing rapid neighborhood growth.
stories.impact: Large-check financing for Brooklyn multifamily signals lender confidence in Gowanus residential fundamentals and continued institutional appetite for NYC rental development. Gowanus neighborhood growth, warehouse conversion trend, and mixed-use development pattern indicate sustained migration to outer-borough, transit-accessible markets; relevant for developers evaluating Brooklyn expansion and waterfront/industrial conversion opportunities. RXR's continued equity participation suggests belief in long-term value appreciation.

stories.title: Greystone Provides HUD/FHA Loan for Central NJ Multifamily
stories.excerpt: Greystone has provided a $47,250,000 HUD/FHA-insured loan for The View at Middlesex, a 200-unit multifamily community in Middlesex, NJ. The financing was originated by John Williams, Drew Fletcher and Bryan Grover. The property was developed by Forte Real Estate Development, a vertically integrated real estate development, investment management, and operations firm. Since 2006, Forte has developed more than 2,000 multifamily units and is among the most active developers in Central New Jersey. The firm specializes in the acquisition, repositioning and development of medium- and large-scale residential properties throughout the state. “The View at Middlesex is a high-quality multifamily asset developed by one of Central New Jersey’s most active and experienced multifamily developers,” said Williams. “HUD-insured financing remains an attractive solution for sponsors seeking long-term, fixed-rate debt, and we were pleased to work alongside Forte Real Estate Development to deliver a financing structure that supports the property’s long-term success.” The post Greystone Provides HUD/FHA Loan for Central NJ Multifamily appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Greystone closed $47.25M HUD/FHA loan for 200-unit View at Middlesex, Central NJ.
Borrower: Forte Real Estate (2,000+ units developed since 2006 in Central NJ).
HUD-insured financing remains attractive for long-term, fixed-rate debt; asset described as high-quality.
stories.summary: Greystone provided a $47.25 million HUD/FHA-insured loan for The View at Middlesex, a 200-unit multifamily asset developed by Forte Real Estate Development. Forte has developed over 2,000 units in Central New Jersey since 2006 and specializes in acquisition, repositioning, and development of medium- to large-scale residential properties. The lender emphasized HUD-insured financing as an attractive long-term, fixed-rate solution.
stories.impact: Central New Jersey multifamily demand and supply remain robust; Forte's track record (2,000+ units) and continued lending appetite signal stable, lower-risk market fundamentals. HUD/FHA financing availability and deployment indicate institutional confidence in Central NJ rents and demographics. Relevant for developers evaluating suburban NJ markets as alternatives to NYC or PA; financing ease for proven local developers suggests lower capital costs and easier path to stabilization in the submarket.

stories.title: D.C. Department of Veterans Affairs Building’s CMBS Loan Enters Special Servicing
stories.excerpt: The $102 million commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) loan on PED Investments’ 425 Eye Street NW, home to the headquarters of the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs’ Office of Construction and Facilities Management, has been sent to special servicing for “imminent monetary default,” according to an alert from Morningstar Credit. The alert notes that the building has a “large exposure” to federal office leases arranged by the federal General Services Administration (GSA), with “the majority (64 percent) of the space expiring this month.” The alert noted that the tenant’s failure to provide a renewal 12 months before expiration was a “trigger event,” although PED Investments did provide a $5.5 million letter of credit. At the time of the alert, PED was “still negotiating with the GSA,” according to Morningstar. A KLNB Washington, D.C., office report from the third quarter of 2021 cited the Department of Veterans Affairs’ lease of 283,242 square feet at the property among its major deals of the quarter. The seven-year loan was originated by Goldman Sachs on Dec. 7, 2021, with an interest rate of 2.94 percent. At the time, Commercial Observer noted that the refinancing for the property — also known as 425 I Street NW — retired $89 million in existing debt and allowed PED to recoup around $9.6 million in equity, as well as establishing an “accretive leasing reserve” of around $2.5 million, according to information from Fitch Ratings. CO also noted that, according to Fitch, Goldman Sachs extended the mortgage at a “conservative” 56.8 percent loan-to-value ratio that was based on an underwritten valuation of $180 million, and that $62.4 million of the loan was set to be securitized in the $625.1 million, Goldman Sachs-led GSMS 2021-GSA3 conduit CMBS deal. A December 2021 release by the Kroll Bond Rating Agency noted that the loan for 425 Eye Street NW was the largest loan in that deal at 10 percent of the total. This was one of two CMBS deals cited by Morningstar
bullets: 425 Eye Street NW (D.C. VA headquarters lease) CMBS loan entered special servicing on $102M deal.
64% of leased space (GSA tenant) expires this month; tenant failure to renew is trigger event.
Property has large federal office lease exposure; lender negotiating on renewal amid distress.
stories.summary: The $102 million CMBS loan on 425 Eye Street NW in Washington, D.C.—home to the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs' Office of Construction and Facilities Management—has been transferred to special servicing due to imminent monetary default. The property has a large concentration (64%) of GSA-leased federal office space expiring immediately, and the tenant's failure to provide renewal notice within 12 months triggered default provisions. The property has a $5.5 million letter of credit, but the lender and GSA are negotiating renewal terms.
stories.impact: Federal office lease concentration risk and GSA lease expirations signal broader Washington, D.C. office market distress. This is primarily an office/federal real estate issue, but it underscores office-to-residential conversion opportunity in D.C. if the VA vacates or downsizes. For multifamily developers, this highlights potential adaptive reuse opportunities in federal office buildings; however, GSA lease negotiations and government tenant retention remain unpredictable, making conversion projects higher-risk than organic multifamily development.

stories.title: Podcast | How CRE is Implementing Agentic Workflows, with Lev CEO Yaakov Zar
stories.excerpt: Yaakov Zar is the founder and CEO of Lev, a software platform built to modernize the workflow of commercial real estate professionals. Yaakov started Lev after experiencing firsthand how broken the CRE financing process was, watching a $4 million loan take six months to close. What began as a tech-enabled brokerage has evolved into a purpose-built agentic workflow platform helping lenders, brokers, and investors manage deals, ingest unstructured data, and move faster. Yaakov is based in New York City. (02:26) Bottom Up vs Top Down (04:31) Slack Origin Tangent (05:59) MetaProp Skills Library (09:43) What Is Defensible AI (11:12) MCP & Rapid Change (12:41) Pilots Everywhere & Demo Fatigue (17:34) Same Workflow, Turbocharged (19:34) Real Estate’s Move 37 Moment (22:04) Why Winning Is Hard to Define (26:07) Lev Agentic Workflows (29:14) Leapfrogging Past Salesforce (31:43) Data Quality Pushback (33:49) Ingesting Email Into CRM (35:54) Selling Software to CRE (39:06) Overhyped AI and Security Risks (42:50) Collaboration Superpower: Steve Jobs Learn more: –Visit Lev –Yaakov on LinkedIn –MetaProp Labs Skills Library Connect with Tangent: -Edward Cohen on LinkedIn -Zach Aarons on LinkedIn -Tangent on LinkedIn Events coming up: –Institutional Investors & PE Forum: in NYC on 9/16 –Data Centers Development & Infrastructure Forum: in NYC on 10/6 –South Florida Capital & Development Forum: in Miami on 10/15 –All upcoming events Disclaimer: Commercial Observer Tangent podcast is for entertainment and informational purposes only, you should not construe any such information or other material as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice. Nothing contained on our program constitutes a solicitation, recommendation, endorsement, or offer by Tangent, Commercial Observer, MetaProp, or any third party guest to buy or sell any securities, public or private, other financial instruments or funds.
bullets: Lev CEO Yaakov Zar discusses agentic AI workflows for CRE financing and deal management.
Platform addresses broken CRE financing (e.g., $4M loan took 6 months to close historically).
Focus: workflow automation, data ingestion, deal velocity for lenders, brokers, and investors.
stories.summary: Podcast featuring Lev CEO Yaakov Zar explores the application of agentic AI workflows to commercial real estate finance and deal management. Zar founded Lev after witnessing inefficiencies in CRE financing processes (e.g., a $4 million loan closing in six months). The platform now helps lenders, brokers, and investors automate workflows, ingest unstructured data, and accelerate deal velocity.
stories.impact: Limited direct relevance to immediate multifamily supply, demand, or capital availability. However, improved CRE financing workflow and deal velocity could reduce closing timelines and transaction friction, indirectly easing access to construction capital for multifamily developers. Faster underwriting and loan processing may lower soft costs and time-to-capital, supporting more rapid deployment of development capital in competitive markets.

stories.title: Luxury Jewelry Brand David Yurman Takes 685 Fifth’s Entire 23K-SF Retail Condo
stories.excerpt: Luxury jewelry retailer David Yurman has leased the entire retail condominium at 685 Fifth Avenue, just one month after a $160 million loan on the property was transferred to special servicing. The jewelry brand will open a new 23,000-square-foot David Yurman flagship store at the base of the 29-story Midtown building, according to landlord GGP, the retail subsidiary of Brookfield Corporation. The property at the corner of Fifth Avenue and 54th Street is also home to 69 luxury residential condos that are part of Michael Shvo’s Mandarin Oriental Residences. The jeweler will take over its new retail space from global fashion house coach, which left 685 Fifth Avenue last month to open a new store nearby at 645 Fifth Avenue, as Commercial Observer first reported. That was right before the $160 million loan on 685 Fifth Avenue, sponsored by GGP, went into special servicing due to concerns of financial default as tenants like Coach let their leases expire and relocate, The Real Deal reported last month. Other tenants at 685 Fifth Avenue include electronics store DJI Hasselblad and footwear brand Stuart Weitzman, both of which have leases expiring within the year. The retail condo was once fully occupied by the Gucci global headquarters. While loan servicer Trimont Real Estate Advisors stuck to claims that the 23,000-square-foot space on Fifth Avenue couldn’t be leased at high rents, GGP was confident in the asset. And, now, it’s fully leased. “High-quality real estate and world-class brands thrive by creating lasting value together. As one of GGP’s long-standing retail partners, David Yurman is a timeless, American luxury jewelry brand, and we look forward to being part of their growing legacy at one of New York’s most iconic corners,” Kevin McCrain, CEO of GGP, said in a statement to CO. It’s unclear whether David Yurman will keep its current New York City flagship store at 5 East 57th Street, which is just three blocks south of its new address. The jewelry brand has sev
bullets: David Yurman leased entire 23,000 SF retail condominium at 685 Fifth Ave, Midtown Manhattan.
New flagship store opens after Coach vacated; property also has 69 luxury residential condos.
Property's $160M CMBS loan entered special servicing due to tenant departures and default risk.
stories.summary: Luxury jewelry brand David Yurman leased the entire retail condominium (23,000 square feet) at 685 Fifth Avenue in Midtown Manhattan for a new flagship store. This fills the space left by Coach, which relocated to 645 Fifth Avenue. The property, owned by GGP (Brookfield's retail subsidiary), also includes 69 luxury residential condos as part of Michael Shvo's Mandarin Oriental Residences. The property's $160 million CMBS loan had just been transferred to special servicing due to tenant departures.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily fundamentals. The residential condo component (Mandarin Oriental) is for-sale, not rental. However, Fifth Avenue retail stabilization (David Yurman backfilling Coach) signals ongoing luxury tenant demand in Midtown, which supports mixed-use projects and ground-floor retail economics in premium NYC submarkets. For multifamily-focused developers, this indicates continued urban vitality in trophy retail corridors, which underpins higher rents in nearby residential markets.

stories.title: Seth Hecht Returns to Colliers From JLL as New Senior Vice President
stories.excerpt: Seth Hecht is returning to Colliers after more than eight years at JLL and another three years at Cushman & Wakefield. Colliers announced Wednesday Hecht’s return as senior vice president on its office leasing team, where he worked from 2006 to 2014. But this time he’s coming back with significant leadership experience, as he previously served as a senior director at C&W and senior vice president at JLL. Hecht will advise both tenants and landlords in their leasing endeavors across New York City, whereas early in his career he assisted in new development strategies, according to Colliers. “The firm has built incredible momentum in New York, and I’m eager to leverage my experience to help clients navigate an evolving market and achieve their goals,” Hecht said in a statement. Hecht will be reporting directly to Colliers Executive Managing Director John Gallagher. “Seth’s return is a testament to the strength of our culture and the strategic investments we’re making across our New York business,” Gallagher said in a statement. “His long-standing industry relationships and market expertise make him an invaluable addition to our team.” Recently, Hecht negotiated a renewal for 26,405 square feet for law firm Sher Tremonte at Princeton International Properties’ 90 Broad Street in April, as well as Tenth Revolution Group in its deal to sublease its 35,454-square-foot office at 199 Water Street, also known as One Seaport Plaza, to fintech firm VCT Holdings in March. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Seth Hecht rejoins Colliers as Senior Vice President on NYC office leasing team.
Hecht returns after 8+ years at JLL and 3 years at Cushman & Wakefield.
Will advise tenants and landlords; reports to Executive Managing Director John Gallagher.
stories.summary: Seth Hecht has rejoined Colliers as Senior Vice President on the New York City office leasing team, after more than eight years at JLL and three years at Cushman & Wakefield. Hecht previously worked at Colliers from 2006 to 2014. In his new role, he will advise both tenants and landlords across NYC leasing transactions, leveraging his experience in new development strategy and market leadership.
stories.impact: Talent move signals Colliers' confidence in NYC market and its competitive positioning for office leasing. Limited direct relevance to multifamily development, though office-to-residential conversion activity in NYC may benefit from Hecht's expertise in landlord negotiations and distressed asset repositioning. Personnel moves of this caliber typically indicate firm-wide investment in growth markets.

stories.title: Churchill seizing Pebb Capital’s Streeterville hotel eyed for apartments conversion in $28M foreclosure
stories.excerpt: Todd Rosenberg’s plan to convert a Streeterville hotel into apartments is ending in a $28 million foreclosure, capping a yearslong saga of financial distress and insider dealmaking for the property at 201 East Delaware Place. Rosenberg’s firm, Boca Raton, Florida-based Pebb Capital, last month agreed to surrender the 18-story building to lender Churchill Real Estate. That company has offices in New York and Charlotte, North Carolina, and has been active in Chicago’s distressed property debt market, according to Cook County Circuit Court records. The hotel, which is out of operation, was known as the Gale Chicago in recent years and […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Churchill Real Estate foreclosing on Streeterville hotel (201 E. Delaware Pl.) for $28M; Pebb Capital surrendered.
Todd Rosenberg's Pebb Capital acquired property to convert to apartments; distress and litigation blocked conversion.
Chicago hotel market still recovering post-pandemic; multiple developers shifting lodging to residential conversion.
stories.summary: Churchill Real Estate is foreclosing on 201 East Delaware Place in Streeterville, Chicago, a former Raffaello/Gale Hotel with an 18-story office/hotel structure. Developer Todd Rosenberg's Pebb Capital, which had acquired the property with plans to convert it to apartments, is surrendering the asset to lender Churchill after months of financial distress and insider dealings. The foreclosure derails residential conversion plans in a Chicago lodging market still struggling with post-pandemic recovery.
stories.impact: Chicago hotel-to-residential conversion trend is stalling due to distressed debt and financing challenges. The Streeterville foreclosure signals that capital may not be readily available for conversion plays in secondary markets, even when underlying residential demand exists. For multifamily developers, this indicates caution on converting older hotel/office stock without strong pre-leasing or capital partner support. Conversely, other Chicago developers (Churchwick Partners, Blackstone entities) are still pursuing conversions, suggesting selective opportunity for well-capitalized sponsors.

stories.title: ZSD bets on family-sized condos in West Loop
stories.excerpt: ZSD is planning a building that features family style condos, with four bedrooms minimum in each unit.  The development, dubbed 1282 Washington on West Washington Boulevard, has pre-sold 12 of the 16 total condos the building will allow for, according to Crain’s. The price per unit sold spanned $1.75 million to $3.2 million, with the remaining four up for a range of $2 million to $3 million. Every unit will have some outdoor space, according to the outlet. The building itself will span five stories. The site was previously owned by a toy company and spans 18,000 square feet.  One […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: ZSD (Chicago developer) pre-sold 12 of 16 family-sized condos at 1282 Washington, West Loop.
Four-bedroom-minimum units; $1.75M–$3.2M per unit sold; all units have outdoor space.
Five-story project on 18,000 SF site (former toy company); completion expected fall 2027; proximity to top schools.
stories.summary: Chicago-based developer ZSD is building 1282 Washington in the West Loop, a five-story condo project with 16 units featuring four-bedroom minimums, outdoor space, and prices ranging from $1.75 million to $3.2 million. ZSD has already pre-sold 12 of 16 units. The developer acquired the 18,000-square-foot site for $5 million in April and received demolition permits in June; construction is expected to complete in fall 2027. Proximity to highly-rated Mark T. Skinner Elementary West is a key marketing anchor.
stories.impact: West Loop condo success and family-unit focus signal sustained demand for large units and neighborhood amenities (schools, walkability) in Chicago's urban core. This is for-sale product, not rental, but the strong pre-leasing pace (75% of units sold pre-construction) indicates robust household formation and affluent demographic inflow. For rental developers, this underscores West Loop's trajectory as a family-friendly urban neighborhood and the demand premium for units with schools nearby—relevant for mixed-income and premium multifamily developments in the area.

stories.title: Brooklyn developer scores financing on one-time David Werner conversion target
stories.excerpt: David Werner’s loss is Hershy Silberstein’s gain. The Blue Sky Builders honcho landed $102 million in construction financing for the office-to-residential conversion of 311 West 43rd Street in Hell’s Kitchen, the Commercial Observer reported. S3 Capital provided the debt for the redevelopment of the Press Building. Silberstein’s involvement in the project wasn’t previously disclosed. Instead, it appeared that one of New York City’s conversion kings, David Werner, would be spearheading the redevelopment. Werner was in contract to buy the 193,000-square-foot building for slightly more than $40 million, The Real Deal reported in April, about one-third of the $131 million that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hershy Silberstein's Blue Sky Builders closed $102M construction financing for 311 W. 43rd St office-to-residential conversion.
S3 Capital provided debt; 160-unit rental + 40,000 SF retail; 467-m tax abatement (25% AMI units).
David Werner's earlier contract deal failed; Silberstein acquired property at undisclosed price; building previously housed WeWork.
stories.summary: Blue Sky Builders' Hershy Silberstein secured $102 million in construction financing for an office-to-residential conversion of 311 West 43rd Street (Press Building) in Hell's Kitchen, New York. The project will deliver 160 rental units and 40,000 square feet of commercial space, with 25% of units designated for residents earning 80% AMI under the 467-m tax abatement program. Silberstein acquired the property after developer David Werner's purchase contract (at slightly over $40 million) failed to close. The building previously housed WeWork before the coworking giant abandoned the space in 2023.
stories.impact: Hell's Kitchen office-to-residential conversion gains momentum with strong construction financing, signaling sustained lender appetite for NYC conversion plays. The property's WeWork legacy and acquisition price (~30% of 2018 purchase value) indicate distressed asset opportunities in NYC office stock. Tax abatement (467-m) significantly improves underwriting for conversion projects with workforce housing components. For developers, this demonstrates that well-capitalized sponsors with development track records (Blue Sky Builders) can access favorable debt terms for conversions, and tax incentives meaningfully improve conversion feasibility in Manhattan.

stories.title: Related Group, BH score approval for Rosewood Residences West Palm Beach
stories.excerpt: Related Group and BH Group’s planned Rosewood Residences in West Palm Beach got the go-ahead. The city of West Palm Beach approved the luxury condo tower at 2001 North Flagler Drive, a BH spokesperson confirmed. The property is next to the site BH and Terra are buying from billionaire Jeff Greene for $100 million, planning a pair of 31-story condo towers. The developers paid $34 million for the 0.8-acre site in 2024. Earlier this year, they filed plans for a 27-story, 90-unit luxury high-rise with more than 13,000 square feet of indoor amenities, a swimming pool on the fifth floor […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related Group and BH Group won city approval for Rosewood Residences, 90-unit luxury condo at 2001 N. Flagler Dr, West Palm Beach.
27-story, 90-unit tower; average unit ~3,400 SF; replacing medical office building; $34M site acquisition in 2024.
Developers also pursuing Ritz-Carlton condo at 1717 N. Flagler (138 units, $200M construction loan) and Hillsboro Beach Rosewood project.
stories.summary: Related Group and BH Group received approval from the City of West Palm Beach for Rosewood Residences, a 27-story, 90-unit luxury condo tower at 2001 North Flagler Drive. Units average approximately 3,400 square feet. The developers acquired the 0.8-acre site in 2024 for $34 million and will replace an existing medical office building. Arquitectonica designed the project, which includes over 13,000 square feet of indoor amenities and a fifth-floor pool. The developers are also partnering on a Ritz-Carlton Residences condo project (138 units, 27 stories) and working on additional Rosewood-branded projects in Hillsboro Beach and Miami Beach.
stories.impact: West Palm Beach condo pipeline expansion signals continued luxury residential demand and developer confidence in South Florida market. Approvals for multiple high-end condo towers (Rosewood, Ritz-Carlton) by leading developers (Related, BH) indicate sustained capital availability and strong buyer demand for luxury product. For multifamily developers, West Palm Beach's 2,000+ units in pipeline reflects broader South Florida residential growth; however, luxury for-sale product dominance suggests higher barriers to entry for rental developers. Opportunity lies in workforce housing and workforce-adjacent rental product, where tax incentives (Live Local Act challenges notwithstanding) may improve feasibility.

stories.title: Miami Design District brings another marquee mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Another mixed-use project designed by a globally recognized architecture firm is coming to the Miami Design District. Miami Design District Associates and Raycliff Capital plan to build Sweetbird North, an eight-story, 120,500-square-foot office-and-retail project that will include more than 92,000 square feet of office space and 22,500 square feet of retail, according to a press release. Construction is expected to kick off in August and wrap up in early 2028. The developer, a joint venture between Craig Robins’ Dacra, L Catterton Real Estate and Brookfield Properties, tapped global architecture firm Snøhetta to design the development which will rise on a […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Design District: Snøhetta-designed Sweetbird North, 8-story, 120,500 SF office/retail project; construction starts August 2024.
92,000 SF office (creative industries), 22,500 SF retail; partners include Dacra, L Catterton, Brookfield.
Completion early 2028; placemaking focus; Design District also developing Miami Design Residences condo by Fouquet's (David Chipperfield architect).
stories.summary: Miami Design District is advancing Sweetbird North, an eight-story, 120,500-square-foot mixed-use project designed by globally-recognized firm Snøhetta. The development will house 92,000 square feet of office space (targeting creative industries) and 22,500 square feet of retail. Developed by Miami Design District Associates (joint venture of Dacra, L Catterton Real Estate, and Brookfield Properties), construction is slated to begin in August 2024 and complete in early 2028. The Design District is also pursuing luxury condo development (Miami Design Residences by Fouquet's) with involvement from Fort Partners and Constellation Hotels.
stories.impact: Miami Design District's mixed-use intensification (office, retail, residential) signals successful placemaking and continued institutional investment in the neighborhood as a cultural and commercial anchor. For multifamily developers, the Design District's trend toward live-work-play products and high-end residential (Fouquet's condo project) underscores demand for experience-driven, walkable urban neighborhoods. However, condo-heavy development suggests limited rental multifamily opportunity in this specific submarket; rental opportunity likely lies in nearby neighborhoods capturing Design District spillover demand.

stories.title: Will Miami commissioners put Live Local Act in legal crosshairs?
stories.excerpt: A Miami official wants the city to take a swing at Florida’s Live Local Act, which would put it in the ring alongside other localities that are punching back at the developer-friendly law. City Commissioner Damian Pardo sponsored a measure to instruct the city attorney to find ways to challenge the law’s validity and authorize him to pursue legal action, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  Florida’s Live Local Act, signed by Gov. Ron DeSantis in 2023, offers tax breaks and zoning incentives to developers that build projects with below market-rate housing. It’s a controversial law that has supporters celebrating […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami City Commissioner Damian Pardo sponsored measure to challenge Florida's Live Local Act validity in court.
Vote scheduled for Thursday; city would authorize legal action against developer-friendly law and tax breaks.
Live Local Act (signed 2023) offers tax incentives and zoning relief for workforce housing; 200+ projects underway statewide.
stories.summary: Miami City Commissioner Damian Pardo has sponsored a measure instructing the city attorney to find legal grounds to challenge Florida's Live Local Act, signed by Governor Ron DeSantis in 2023. The law provides tax breaks and zoning incentives to developers building below-market-rate housing. Miami commissioners will vote on the challenge measure Thursday. Pardo argues the law undermines local planning authority and neighborhood character, while opponents contend it expands workforce housing access. Hollywood and Hillsborough County have also filed legal challenges; over 200 Live Local Act projects are in development statewide.
stories.impact: Live Local Act legal challenges create regulatory uncertainty for South Florida multifamily developers relying on tax incentives and zoning flexibility for workforce housing projects. If Miami succeeds in blocking or narrowing the law, project underwriting assumptions (cost offsets, density bonuses) could shift materially, requiring re-underwriting or re-entitlement. For developers with Live Local Act projects in Miami, legal delays and potential policy rollback increase execution risk and capital deployment timelines. Conversely, if the law survives challenge, it remains an attractive subsidy mechanism for mixed-income rental development across Florida—critical tool given housing affordability pressures and labor cost inflation in South Florida.
